title: UCB — Epilepsy — Thesis
html:
UCB Epilepsy Thesis Tracker — Vinoth's POV

TA: Neurology — Epilepsy (Briviact, Fintepla, Nayzilam) Initiated: 2026-04-25 Last reviewed: 2026-04-25 Review cadence: Quarterly (next: July 2026)

How to use this document

Each thesis is a forward-looking bet — not a fact summary. The value is in being wrong and knowing why you were wrong. Update quarterly:

  • Did new evidence strengthen or weaken the thesis?
  • What would you tell a UCB stakeholder if they asked your view on this?
  • Are there ZoomRx study design implications?

Thesis 1: Fintepla's growth ceiling is set by the comfort gap, not the efficacy data

The bet: Fintepla has BETTER clinical data than Epidiolex (4x vs 3x drug response in DS, 3x vs 2x in LGS) but trails on prescriber comfort (~40% comfortable starting a patient vs significantly higher for Epidiolex). The brand that closes the comfort gap wins. Until UCB solves the REMS/cardiac monitoring friction, Fintepla will grow but never overtake Epidiolex, regardless of how strong the efficacy messaging is.

Why this matters for Vinoth: ZoomRx's SFE study is designed to track exactly this dynamic — message effectiveness, comfort to start, and awareness of support programs (At-Home ECHO, AES). If comfort shifts wave-over-wave, we're measuring the right thing. If it doesn't, we need to push UCB to rethink study design toward barrier-based deep dives rather than continued message testing.

Evidence FOR:

  • At-Home ECHO awareness grew from ~40% to ~48% between Wave 1 and Wave 2
  • AES awareness grew from ~20% to ~33%
  • Likelihood to recommend improved for both LGS and DS
  • HCP verbatims confirm: when At-Home ECHO is communicated, the monitoring barrier drops
  • Likelihood to increase Rx for DS is now HIGHER for Fintepla than Epidiolex (Q1 2026) — the comfort gap may be narrowing

Evidence AGAINST:

  • Comfort with starting a patient has not meaningfully moved between waves despite improved support awareness
  • The gap could be structural (REMS is a regulatory requirement, not a messaging problem)
  • Epidiolex is expanding its own support programs and has the TSC indication providing additional prescribing volume and familiarity

Confidence: High. This thesis will sharpen with Wave 3 data (expected ~Q3 2026). If comfort stays flat while awareness grows, the barrier is deeper than communication.

What to watch: Wave 3 SFE data — comfort metric. At-Home ECHO utilization data (if available from UCB). Any REMS modifications from FDA.

Thesis 2: Briviact's messaging has peaked — the brand needs a new commercial narrative or it fades to generic

The bet: Briviact's message effectiveness scores have been declining across waves (Q4 2024 → Q1 2025). The "#1 prescribed branded ASM" (B2) message is losing recall rank. Motivation and believability are dropping across the full message set. Meanwhile, XCOPRI is growing 50%+ YoY with an aspirational "zero seizures" narrative. Without a fundamentally new commercial story, Briviact becomes a messaging optimization exercise on a declining trajectory — and then generics arrive.

Why this matters for Vinoth: ZoomRx is running quarterly PET for Briviact vs XCOPRI. If message fatigue is real, ongoing PET becomes a "diminishing returns" measurement — we'll keep showing the same pattern. The consulting opportunity shifts from "optimize messages" to "reimagine positioning." That's a higher-value conversation with UCB.

Evidence FOR:

  • Q1 2025: ME scores dropped significantly across all Briviact messages; motivation and believability both declined
  • XCOPRI message recall improved significantly between Q4 2024 and Q1 2025, while Briviact's was flat-to-declining
  • XCOPRI SOV (39%) nearly double Briviact (21%) — and XCOPRI launched its first TV campaign in May 2026
  • BRIVIACT visit frequency dropped (1.9 → 1.2) while reach held steady, suggesting rep investment is being pulled back
  • Generic brivaracetam oral solution approved (Lupin, Feb 2026)

Evidence AGAINST:

  • Briviact still grew 14% globally in 2025 — revenue isn't declining yet
  • The B7 (long-term retention) and B3 (SGTC seizure freedom when started earlier) messages still rate above others — there may be specific messages worth doubling down on
  • BRIVIACT reps improved significantly on rep closing (asking for the Rx) from Q4 to Q1
  • The SV2A differentiation story vs Keppra remains clinically valid — it may just need repackaging

Confidence: Medium-high. The generic timeline will determine whether this is a slow decline or a cliff. If UCB invests in a "refresh" (new messaging platform, new data, new positioning), this thesis weakens.

What to watch: Q2 2025 PET data. UCB investor calls for any Briviact repositioning signals. Generic tablet launch timing. Whether UCB shifts Briviact sales force resources to Fintepla.

Thesis 3: CDKL5 approval will validate Fintepla as a rare epilepsy platform, not just a two-indication drug

The bet: The GEMZ trial success (47.6% vs 2.8% seizure reduction) in CDKL5 deficiency disorder is more strategically important than the incremental revenue it generates. CDD is ultra-rare (~1,000 US patients). But getting a third indication transforms UCB's story from "Fintepla is a DS+LGS treatment" to "Fintepla is THE rare epilepsy platform with a unique MOA." This narrative shift will: (a) strengthen the MoA message (FL11/FD11 — already the most effective message), (b) increase prescriber confidence in the drug broadly, and (c) create a consulting opportunity for ZoomRx to study label expansion impact on existing indications.

Why this matters for Vinoth: If UCB files and gets approval in 2026-2027, they'll likely want: (1) pre-launch tracking in CDD, (2) cross-indication message testing, (3) launch effectiveness tracking. This is a BD opportunity to expand the Fintepla workstream.

Evidence FOR:

  • GEMZ results are compelling (47.6% vs 2.8%) — strongest efficacy signal across all three indications
  • No approved treatment for CDD currently exists — first-mover advantage
  • Same REMS, same At-Home ECHO infrastructure — marginal operational cost
  • UCB said they'll file "as soon as possible"
  • The MoA message is already Fintepla's most effective message — a third indication reinforces this

Evidence AGAINST:

  • Ultra-rare population means limited revenue impact
  • Filing ≠ approval — FDA review timelines are uncertain
  • CDD patients are younger (onset in first weeks of life) and may need different dosing / safety data
  • Attention on CDD filing could distract from DS/LGS commercial execution

Confidence: Medium. The clinical data is strong. The strategic narrative value is clear. The question is timing and whether UCB invests in CDD launch vs just adding a label line.

What to watch: UCB regulatory filing announcement. FDA review designation (Priority Review vs standard). Any UCB inquiries to ZoomRx about CDD tracking studies.

Thesis 4: The Nayzilam-Valtoco battle is lost on distribution, not on clinical merit

The bet: Nayzilam is clinically better-positioned than VALTOCO (simpler dosing, higher rep quality ratings, better message recall vs matched themes). But VALTOCO's commercial machine ($300M in 2025, doubled from 2024) and broader age range (>=2 years vs >=12 years) create a structural advantage that message optimization cannot overcome. Without a pediatric label expansion, Nayzilam caps out as a niche player in adult/adolescent rescue therapy.

Why this matters for Vinoth: ZoomRx runs PET for Nayzilam vs VALTOCO. If the thesis is correct, the PET study is measuring Nayzilam winning on message quality while losing on market share — a dynamic that's interesting but not actionable through messaging changes alone. The consulting insight should shift toward distribution/access/pediatric strategy.

Evidence FOR:

  • VALTOCO: $295-300M in 2025 (doubled from 2024); 12,000+ new pediatric prescriptions after age expansion
  • VALTOCO rep reach: 87% vs Nayzilam 72%
  • VALTOCO SOV dominance in the acute seizure cluster space
  • Nayzilam's age restriction (>=12) excludes a large population of pediatric rescue therapy need
  • VALTOCO patents extend to 2032

Evidence AGAINST:

  • Nayzilam message effectiveness is higher than VALTOCO across matched themes
  • Nayzilam reps outperform VALTOCO reps on overall sales call quality
  • Nayzilam's simple dosing (1 device = 1 dose, no weight calculation) is a genuine clinical advantage
  • If Nayzilam gains a pediatric indication, the competitive dynamic shifts
  • Midazolam has pharmacokinetic advantages (faster onset, shorter half-life) that matter clinically

Confidence: Medium-high. This thesis holds unless UCB secures a pediatric expansion.

What to watch: Any UCB clinical trial activity for Nayzilam in pediatric patients (<12 years). VALTOCO quarterly revenue reports. Market share shifts in the rescue space.

Thesis 5: UCB's Neurona acquisition signals a 5-10 year epilepsy pipeline bet that reframes the entire relationship

The bet: The $1.15B Neurona Therapeutics acquisition (April 2026) is a signal that UCB is building toward a next-generation epilepsy portfolio — interneuron cell therapy (NRTX-1001) for drug-resistant epilepsy. This is NOT incremental. If cell therapy for epilepsy works, it changes the treatment paradigm from chronic medication to one-time curative therapy. For ZoomRx, this means the UCB relationship evolves: today we track ASM sales effectiveness; in 5-10 years, we might be tracking cell therapy adoption, surgeon referral patterns, or treatment center selection.

Why this matters for Vinoth: This is a long-term BD thesis, not a near-term delivery thesis. But it informs how Vinoth positions the UCB account strategically — UCB is not just maintaining an epilepsy portfolio, they're building one. Every conversation with UCB should acknowledge this ambition.

Evidence FOR:

  • $1.15B is a significant bet for UCB (not a small bolt-on)
  • NRTX-1001 is in clinical trials for drug-resistant mesial temporal lobe epilepsy
  • UCB's stated revenue guidance (high single-digit to low double-digit growth for 2026) suggests confidence in the existing portfolio funding future investments
  • Drug-resistant epilepsy is a massive unmet need (30-40% of all epilepsy patients)

Evidence AGAINST:

  • Cell therapy for epilepsy is early-stage — Phase 1/2 at best
  • 5-10 year timeline means enormous uncertainty
  • Manufacturing complexity for cell therapies is orders of magnitude higher than small molecules
  • Regulatory pathway for neurological cell therapy is uncharted

Confidence: Low-medium (on timeline). High (on strategic intent). The bet here is about positioning, not prediction.

What to watch: NRTX-1001 clinical data readouts. UCB investor presentations for epilepsy pipeline updates. Any ZoomRx inbound inquiries from UCB about pipeline tracking or pre-launch studies.
